Duff, Jeremy. The Elements of New Testament Greek. New York: Cambridge University Press, 2005.

Linguistics and grammar:
Black, David Alan. Linguistics for Students of New Testament Greek. Grand Rapids: Baker Books, 1995.
Herbert Weir Smyth. A Greek Grammar for Colleges. American Book Company, 1920.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Introduction to the Hebrew Bible
courses.description: This course orientates students to the diverse scholarship which encompasses the study of the Hebrew Scripture: from archaeology, historical-critical, grammatico-historical, socio-historical methodologies to the history of composition of the biblical books, languages, religions and literature of the period before the common era. Inevitably, it also introduces the world of the ancient Near East – its history, religions and literature, which the Hebrew Scripture belonged and related to. This course traces the challenges confronted bythe nation of Israel from its beginning up until the period of the Second Temple.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
For students in Master of Divinity
Exclusion: THEO5919 The Hebrew Bible in Contexts
courses.learning_outcomes:
Knowledge Outcomes:
1. The students will know and understand
a. the general overview of the contents in the Hebrew Bible
b. the direct relationship of the composition of the books to her contexts
c. a general grasp of the scholarship pertaining to the study of the Hebrew Bible
2. The students can describe, explain and discuss
a. the main issues related to the composition and redaction of the books in the Hebrew Bible
b. the main issues of critical scholarship concerning the origin and identity of Israel
c. the proposed historical contexts of the Hebrew Bible
d. the relationships or/and tensions between the traditions underlying the theological motivations and the contemporary
e. situation/s of the text

Attitude Outcomes:
The students will appreciate
a. the making/compilation of the Hebrew Bible
b. the necessity for language acquisition in the study of the Hebrew Bible
c. the diverse research faculties in the study of the Hebrew Bible
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction: Course Introduction; Introduction: Imaginative Remembering; Literature and Genre
2. Torah (1): Introduction to Torah Hebrew Bible as (Christian) Scripture
3. Torah (2): Genesis; Exodus 1-18 Myth, Legend and ANET/Archaeology
4. Torah (3): Exodus 25-40, Leviticus, Number 1-10; Cult and Priestly school
5. Torah (4): Exodus 19-24, Numbers 11-36, Deuteronomy; Law and Narrative
6. Prophets (1): Introduction to the Prophets, Joshua, Judges; History and Deuteronomistic History
7. Prophets (2): Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel; Literary Prophet and Their Social World
8. Prophets (3): 1 and 2 Samuel, 1 and 2 Kings; Narrative and Ideology
9. Prophets (4): Minor Prophets; Twelve prophet as a book
10. Writings (1): Introduction to the Writings, Psalms; The Canonical Study of the Psalms
11. Writings (2): Job, Proverbs; Wisdom Literature
12. Writings (3): Five Scrolls, Ezra-Nehemiah; Hebrew Bible and the early Judaism
13. Writings (4)/ Conclusion: Daniel, 1 and 2 Chronicles; Apocalyptic Literature and the Rewritten Bible; Concluding Reflection: Hiddenness of God
courses.feedback_evaluation: An end-of-term university-wide course evaluation will be conducted in class. Students’ comments and feedback on the course through e-mail or personal meetings with instructor and/or tutors are always welcomed.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Introduction to the New Testament
courses.description: The main purpose of this course is to give students a general understanding the text of the New Testament, its methods of studies, its background and the issues involved. To achieve this purpose, it is necessary for students to know how the NT is interpreted in the light of modern thoughts. This involves firstly a good knowledge of the meaning of the basic text of the NT - this is in fact the prerequisite condition for students who are going to take this course; those do not know the text of the NT have to read through it twice (both students who are going to take this course; those do not know the text of the NT have to read through it twice (both Chinese and English version) at least. Besides, students are also required to read some basic works of modern interpretation of the NT.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
For students in Master of Divinity
Exclusion THEO5910 New Testament in Contexts
courses.learning_outcomes:
Knowledge Outcomes:
1. To give students a general understanding the text of the New Testament, its methods of studies, its background and the issues involved; and
2. To be able to grasp the method of Bible study and exegesis, particularly Pauline Letters.
Skills Outcomes:
1. To enhance critical intelligence thinking; and
2. To enhance analytical ability throughout exegesis.
Attitude Outcomes
1. To develop one’s ability to have a critical and yet sympathetic understanding and appreciation of different church traditions and scholarly perspectives; and
2. To develop one’s sensitivity to the limit of doctrines and enhance openness to different exegesis.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. History of the New Testament
- Investigate the emergence of New Testament and how it became a cannon of Christianity.
2. Contexts of New Testament
- Investigate the social, political, economic, philosophical and literary context of New Testament and how they affect the development of the text.
3. New Testament Criticisms
- Investigate the basic ideas and methodology of major historical criticisms of New Testament
4. Interpretation of New Testament
- Basic interpretation method in interpreting New Testament in light of the contexts
5. Main theme of different books
- Introducing the basic thoughts of various books in New Testament
courses.feedback_evaluation: Feedback and recommendation are welcome. Students are advised to either give feedback during lecture or through email. Response will be made promptly based on the need of students.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Wisdom and the Psalms
courses.description: This course studies the “wisdom” genre of the Hebrew Bible/Old Testament, namely Job, Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, Ecclesiasticus and the Wisdom of Solomon, and some wisdom passages in the prophetic genre, as well as the book of Psalms. It studies the concept of “wisdom” in these books, and their distinction from, and relation to the other books of the Hebrew Bible/Old Testament. At the same time, it also considers its relationship to the ancient Near East genre of instruction literature.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Divinity
courses.learning_outcomes:
Knowledge Outcomes:
1. The students will know and understand
a. the historical background to the wisdom literature as genre in the Old Testament and the general scholarship
b. the rudimentary of Hebrew poetry
c. the general scholarship pertaining to scholarship in Psalms
2. The students can describe, explain and evaluate
a. the major literary features of wisdom books and the division in the Books of Psalms
b. the major themes, purposes and historical contexts and relevance of the individual books
c. the relationship between Psalms, the wisdom literature and the other genres and books of the Old Testament
d. the relevance of these books to the world today

Attitude Outcomes:
The students will appreciate:
a. the relevance of this genre to other cultures and their distinctions
b. the emotional and spiritual livelihood of Israel in relation to their deity as they confront political and societal challenges
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Course Introduction, What is Wisdom?
2. Overview on Wisdom Literature Scholarship, Wisdom Theology; Hebrew Poetry and Parallelism
3. .The Book of Proverbs (I)
4. The Book of Proverbs (II);
5. Qoheleth (I)
6. Qoheleth (II)
7. Job (I)
8. Job (II)
9. Introduction on Psalms
10. Psalms (I)
11. Psalms (II)
12. Psalms (III)
13. Concluding Discussion
courses.feedback_evaluation: A tailored made mid-term course evaluation and end-of-term university wide course evaluation will be conducted in class. Students’ comments and feedback on the course through e-mail or within Blackboard or personal meeting with instructor are always welcomed.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Johannine Literature
courses.description: This course will include a study of the authorship, purpose and main themes of the Johannine literature. Special attention will be given to some selected passages from the Gospel according to John, the Epistles of John and Revelation (Apocalypse).
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Divinity
courses.learning_outcomes:
Knowledge Outcomes:
1. To have basic knowledge of the content, theology and historical contexts of Johannine Literature (including Apocalypse);
2. To have basic knowledge of the so-called "Johannine Community";
3. To be able to understand particularly the Gospel of John (or the Apocalypse); and
4. To be able to grasp the method of Bible study and exegesis, particularly Johannine Literature.

Skills Outcomes:
1. To enhance critical intelligence thinking; and
2. To enhance analytical ability throughout exegesis.

Attitude Outcomes:
1. To develop one’s ability to have a critical and yet sympathetic understanding and appreciation of different church traditions and

2. To develop one’s sensitivity to the limit of doctrines and enhance openness to different exegesis.
courses.course_syllabus:
• Issues in Critical Studies of the Johannine Literature in the NT
• The Gospel of John (Literary Features, Christology, Social Historical Contexts, Jewish-Christian Hostility, Theological Views, Faith and Community, Resurrection and Mission)
• The Johannine Epistles (1 John: Tradition and Schism, 2 John: Leadership and Conflict, 3 John: Hospitality and Isolation)
• The Book of Revelation (Literary Genre and Visionary Rhetoric, Social Historical Contexts, Visions in Heaven, Visions on Earth, Visions of New Haven, New Earth and New City)
• Legacy of the Johannine Literature (Gnosticism, Community, Hope, How to Interpret it today)
courses.feedback_evaluation:
Students are encouraged to share their learning experience throughout the course.
Student’s weekly oral presentations of critical reviews and exegesis-research in class provide concrete and early feedback for the progress of the course and for the adjustment of its pedagogy before the course is finished.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: The book of Ezra-Nehemiah and the books of Chronicles
courses.description: This course focuses on the books of Ezra, Nehemiah and Chronicles in the Hebrew Bible, which are important parts of Hebrew Bible Historiography reflecting the situation of the Jews in the Second Temple Period. The scholarship associated with the Second Temple/Persian Period is also soaring in new and exciting frontier which we hope this course can help students to follow such a trend. Issues including textual criticism, authorship and readership, sources, theme and theology will be discussed.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Divinity
courses.course_syllabus:
Second Temple Period: To call the time when the post-exilic Israelites were ruled under the Persian empire Second Temple Period is to highlight the centrality of the rebuilt temple toward the development of the ideologies and theologies in that period. We will investigate why and how the Second Temple Period was a time when Jewish literary activities were most vibrant and the formative period that the early Judaism and Christianity’s main ideas emerged. The matter on how to call this important period in the Jewish history will be raised: Second Temple period is better than the term “intertestamental period” which traditionally regards as a time of silent in Divine revelation.

Persian colonization and Yehud province: In order to establish the historical and sociological context of the biblical books, the history of Persian empire and its colonization program will be introduced and discussed. Yehud as a province of Persian colonization and its significance in the Jewish self-understand and political make-up will be discussed.

Different Social Identities of the Jews: Exclusive and Inclusive Jewish Community: We will look into the different communities in the Second Temple Period, namely the diaspora in different region, the Golah in Babylonian soil, the returnee and “the people of the land”/local population. We will examine how these groups formulated and articulated their ideologies within the Hebrew Bible and their viewpoints on various issues, especially on these communities’ manner toward outsiders, either they were inclined toward an exclusive policy or a more inclusive one.

Aramaic Sections of the Hebrew Bible: Parts of the Hebrew Bible are written in Aramaic, the lingua franca in the Second Temple Period. We will study the Aramaic section within the book of Ezra and discuss the reason behind this diglossia phenomenon. As a result, we will learn how different scholars reconstruct the history behind these Aramaic texts within the Hebrew Bible.

Temple worship and Temple Personnel in the Persian Period: As a new temple were rebuilt during the Persian period, it resulted in a re-installment of temple worship in the Jewish life. The various topics regarding this theme, from the meaning of temple worship in before and after the exile to the change of importance and activities of different temple personnel in the post-exilic time, will be explored during the course.

Prophecy in the Second Temple period: Did prophecy cease during the Second Temple Period? How the old prophecies were interpreted during this time? This part of the course explore the interpretative activities regarding to the divine messages in the Second Temple Period.

The new understanding of Historiography and its ideology: The writing of a nation’s past can take many forms. We will discuss how the book of Ezra-Nehemiah as a Autobiographical Memoir and the book of Chronicle as a Utopia Literature contribute to the new understanding of historiography in the scholarly world.

Ezra-Nehemiah-Chronicles as Christian Scripture: Various themes relating to Christian theology such as Retribution, Restoration, Canonization and the Pastoral aspect of the biblical books concerning our course will be explored.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Two course evaluations will be conducted. The first will be done midway through the course which is tailored to facilitate better progress through the course. The second will be conducted by the university wide exercise.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Early and Medieval Church
courses.description: The purpose of the course is to introduce the students to the historical and theological developments in the early and medieval church, from Jesus to the dawn of the Reformation and to engage them in a critical dialogue with earlier forms of contextualization of the Christian faith.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Divinity
courses.learning_outcomes:
a) The students will gain a general knowledge of the theological and ecclesial development during the covered period.
b) The students become familiar with key doctrinal development and the interplay between religious institutions and socio-political circumstances of the early and medieval period of the church.
c) The students will get acquainted with different perspectives on church history and with issues that are still debated.
d) The students will develop sensitivity regarding the diversity of Christian faith expressions and gain historical depth in their theological reflection.
e) The students learn about historical forms of contextualization.
f) The critical study of church history helps the students to recover the voices of those who have been condemned as heresy.
courses.course_syllabus:
Session 1:
Introduction to the course
The church in the first two centuries
• What is the Relation of Jesus to the Church?
• Steps in the Development of the Early Church and the spread of Christianity
• The Separation between the Church and the Jewish Community

Session 2: Christianity and politics
Conflicts with the State
The extension of Christianity in the first three centuries

Session 3: Challenges to the faith in the second century and the development of early Catholic theology
The pre-catholic church
Gnostic crisis, Marcionism and the Montanist crisis
Response to the challenges to the faith: Development of catholic theology

Session 4: Christianity and culture: The encounter with Hellenist philosophy
Hellenistic philosophies
Patristic theology
The early Trinitarian debates

Session 5: Christianity becoming the religion of the empire
The so-called Constantinian conversion
Religious policy after Constantine
Constantine’s impact on the religious life of the catholic church
Impact on the theology of the catholic church
Counter-movements to the imperial church: Ascetism and monasticism

Session 6: Theological and ecclesial controversies of the 4th century
Donatism
The Arian controversy until the Council of Nicea
The continuation of the controversy until Constantinople

Session 7: Theology and christological debates in the 5th century
The Council of Chalcedon
Augustine

Session 8: Mission history: the extension of Christianity and its inculturation in new contexts
The Encounter of the catholic church with non-Roman kingdoms
Shifts in world Christianity and the confrontation with Islam

Session 9: Church and state: The growth of papal and ecclesial power
The history of the papal claim of primacy
The development of the secular power of the popes
The conflict between the papacy and the Empire
Medieval papacy, church and state

Session 10: The history of a growing division: The churches in East and West on separate ways
Christological debates of the 6th and 7th century
The iconoclastic debate

Session 11: The history of the other church: Revivalism, reform, and alternative
Christian faith expression in medieval times
Christian mysticism
Monastic reform movements
Mendicant orders
Radical and heretical movements
The Catholic Church and the ‘other church’

Session 12: Outward militancy and inward divisions in high and late medieval times
The crusades
The inquisition
The Babylonian captivity of the church and the divided papacy
The conciliar church reform movement

Session 13: Theological Developments during the Medieval Time
Representatives, forms and historical development of medieval theology
The controversy about the universals
The controversy between rationalism and voluntarism
courses.feedback_evaluation: An end-of-term university-wide course evaluation will be conducted in class. Students’ comments and feedback on the course through e-mail or personal meetings with instructor and/or tutors are always welcomed.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: History of Christianity in China
courses.description: This course aims at giving the students a macro historical picture of Christianity in China from the seventh century to the present. Special attention will be paid to the interaction between Christianity and the Chinese society.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Divinity
courses.learning_outcomes:
Learning outcomes
(1) To assist students to understand the historical development of Christianity in China
(2) To assist students to acquaint the methods of conducting research on Chinese church history
Attitude Outcomes
(1) To develop and affirm students’ identity towards Chinese church history
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Voices from the Past1: Writing the History of Christianity in China
2. Experience: Empiricist Research on Christianity in Contemporary China
3. Preface: Traces of Early Christian Encounters
4. Friendship: Images of the Jesuits in Imperial China
5. The Task: The “Protestant Plan” for China
6. Sisters: Portraits of Women in Chinese Christianity
7. Culture: The Story of 19th-century Chinese Protestantism
8. Contextualization: In Search of an Indigenous Chinese Christianity
9. Conflict: Remembering the Anti-Christian Movement
10. Officials: Tracking Chinese Christianity vis-à-vis CCP rule
11. Perceptions: Histories of Contemporary Chinese Christianities
12. Student Presentations: proposals
courses.feedback_evaluation: An end-of-term university-wide course evaluation will be conducted in class. Students’ comments and feedback on the course through e-mail or personal meetings with instructor and/or tutors are always welcomed.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: History of Christianity in Asia
courses.description: The course introduces the students to the history of Christianity in Asia, from the first century and the mission to India through the Sassanid and the Mongolian period, the spread of Roman-Catholic and Protestant faith in the colonial era to the development of independent Christianity since the 20th century. A significant focus of the course will be on more recent developments in different parts of Asia.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Divinity
courses.learning_outcomes:
a) The students will gain an understanding of the historical development of Christianity in Asia and will learn to perceive and appreciate Christianity in Asia as a partly parallel and independent rather than as latter development to Christianity in Europe.
b) The students will learn about the contemporary challenges of Christian churches in different contexts of Asia and the reasons for different growth developments. They will learn to distinguish between joint Asian concerns and the concerns of particular contexts.
c) In the different historical developments of Christianity in Asia, the students will encounter different models of Christianity and Christian relations to their cultural environment. The students will equally discover different issues of contextualization.
d) The students will develop sensitivity regarding mission, ecumenism, and contextualization in the postcolonial Asian context and learn about different Christian responses to the multi-religious context of Asia. The focus on the diversity of Christian churches and faith expressions in Asia leads beyond the dualism of local church and church in the West and helps perceive Christianity in a multi-polar world.
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1: Introduction to the course
Week 2: The spread of Christianity in the first 10 centuries
Week 3: Asian Christianity from 1000 to 1800
Week 4: The emergence of the Protestant mission movement
Week 5: Protestant Christianity across 19th century Asia
Week 6: Korean Christianity
Week 7: Christianity and Buddhism in Vietnam, Laos and Cambodia
Week 8: Christianity in Thailand
Week 9: Christianity in Myanmar
Week 10: Christianity facing Islam
Week 11: Christianity in India and Nepal
Week 12: Global faith movements in the Asian context
Week 13: Conclusions
courses.feedback_evaluation: End-of-term university wide course evaluation will be conducted in class

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Systematic Theology I
courses.description: Systematic theology can be understood as the theological discipline that constructively articulates the meaning, coherence, and implications of Christian beliefs. This course aims at providing students with a solid methodological foundation for the study of systematic theology by investigating various issues that belong to the “prolegomena” of systematic theology, including the formative factors of theology (such as revelation, Bible, tradition, experience, reason, and culture), theological language, and theological methods.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Divinity
courses.learning_outcomes: After completing this course, the student is expected (a) to have an historical overview of the major paradigms in Christian theology; (b) to understand significant issues and diverse views regarding the formative factors of theology (especially the Bible, but also tradition, culture, theological language, etc.); (c) to have basic knowledge of exemplary theological systems and methods; (d) to be able to think analytically and critically in the field of theology.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. What Is Systematic Theology?
2. Emergence of Christian Theology in the Patristic Era
3. Medieval and Reformation Theology
4. Theology in Modernity
5. Theological Language
6. Revelation and Jesus Christ
7. Scripture (1)
8. Scripture (2)
9. Tradition
10. Experience and Reason
11. Culture and Society
12. Theological Systems and Methods (1)
13. Theological Systems and Methods (2)
courses.feedback_evaluation: An end-of-term university-wide course evaluation will be conducted in class. Students’ comments and feedback on the course through e-mail or personal meetings with instructor and/or tutors are always welcomed.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Systematic Theology II
courses.description: After completing this course, the student is expected (a) to a solid understanding of major Christian doctrines, including the diversity of views, major historical developments, and significant issues involved, and their interrelations; (b) to be able to read and understand the writings of some important theologians, especially those in the patristic era, the Reformation era, and the twentieth century; (c) to be able to critically articulate the relevance of systematic theology for Christian life and churches.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Divinity
courses.learning_outcomes: After completing this course, the student is expected (a) to a solid understanding of major Christian doctrines, including the diversity of views, major historical developments, and significant issues involved, and their interrelations; (b) to be able to read and understand the writings of some important theologians, especially those in the patristic era, the Reformation era, and the twentieth century; (c) to be able to critically articulate the relevance of systematic theology for Christian life and churches.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction / God
2. Creation, Providence, and Evil (1)
3. Creation, Providence, and Evil (2)
4. Humanity, Sin, and Grace
5. The Person and Work of Christ (1)
6. The Person and Work of Christ (2)
7. Christian Life and the Holy Spirit
8. Holy Spirit and the Trinity
9. Church (1): Nature and Ministry
10. Church (2): Sacraments
11. Church (3): The Church in the World
12. Christian Hope and the Kingdom of God
13. Theology of Religions
courses.feedback_evaluation: An end-of-term university-wide course evaluation will be conducted in class. Students’ comments and feedback on the course through e-mail or personal meetings with instructor and/or tutors are always welcomed.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Homiletics

meetings.instructor:
Dr. LI Kwan Hung Leo, 
Professor KWAN Shui Man

courses.description:
This course will cover: 1) the principles, construction and technique in preaching; and
2) the content and message of a sermon in contemporary world. This course will include video-taping students’ preaching. Tutorials after preaching will be conducted for the improvement of technique and content.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Divinity
courses.learning_outcomes:
To understand the principle of preaching on theological topics.
To understand the integration of different types of preaching and the way of preparation.
To learn and practice the techniques and styles in delivering a sermon.
courses.course_syllabus:
(一) 課程評估
分享／引論
聽道／講道經驗及心得
何謂講道學（Homiletics）？
講道的構成因素——真道、表達、使命、講者、聽眾、技巧、權柄
(二) 真道
何謂道？
聖經與道
宣講與教導之不同
道與行
(三) 講章的分類
專題式講道
經文式講道
釋經講道
(四) 講章的性質
佈道
培靈
奮興
牧養
婚禮
喪追思
紀念日
聖曆及特別主日
(五) 經文的選擇與運用
用一段經文的好處
選擇經文要留意的地方
如何運用經文
(六) 如何決定主旨
主旨的必要
主旨的選擇
(七) 如何決定題目
(八) 講章的結構
分段與大綱
引論、本論、結論
(九) 導言
三個目的
(十) 例證
講道的天窗
(十一) 結論與應用
應用的重要性
應用的三個成份
應用的動機
(十二) 講章的準備和組成的步驟
探索期
專注期
講章的組合
最後的準備
(十三) 講道的技巧
講者的素質與風格
講章的表達與演繹
(十四) 試講
courses.feedback_evaluation: An end-of-term university-wide course evaluation will be conducted in class. Students’ comments and feedback on the course through e-mail or personal meetings with instructor and/or tutors are always welcomed.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Liturgics
courses.description: The course aims at equipping students with the understanding of the history and theology of the sacred worship, which include the development of the sacred worship, the notion of liturgical time, of ecclesiastical year, of sacred space, of Monastic and Cathedral office, of the liturgy of the hours. It also helps student to gain a critical understanding on various liturgical terms, liturgical vessels, books and vestments. Contextualization of liturgy in Chinese/HK contexts will also be discussed.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Divinity
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. students will gain critical understanding on the history and theology of sacred worship.
2. students will be able to apply the knowledge gain from this course to their own ecclesiastical context.
3. students will acquire basic but core skills in appropriately performing liturgies.
4. students will be able to develop their own visions on how the liturgical traditions should be contextualized in the Chinese context
courses.course_syllabus:
I Corporate Worship and Church Life; Worship in Truth and Spirit; Worship as Dialogue.
II. Design and Discharge in Worship. The Study of 1 Corinthians 14; The Planning of the Order of Service; Writing of corporate prayers and mediation for the lectionary.
III Worship as Offering; Old Testament Sacrifices, Levitical Priesthood and the Church as a Royal Priesthood.
IV. Time as Communication: The Christian Calendar; the Lectionary；Reading the Bible/ Preaching According to the Lectionary in the Service.
V. Space as Communication; Christian Art and Worship.
VI. Worship as Pastoral Care: The Funeral and the Wedding Service; Special Occasions.
VII. The Sacraments and Corporate Worship—The Rite of Initiation-Holy Baptism.
VIII. The Sacraments and Corporate Worship—The Rite of Continuation— Eucharist.
IX. Worship as a Drama—The Western Roman Catholic tradition; Vatican II Liturgical Reform; Worship as a Journey--The Orthodox Church tradition.
X. The Protestant Tradition: The Normative and Regulative Principles of Liturgical Reforms: Reformed, Lutheran, Anglican.
XI. The Function of Congregational Singing in Corporate Worship; Hymnology
courses.feedback_evaluation: An end-of-term university-wide course evaluation will be conducted in class. Students’ comments and feedback on the course through e-mail or personal meetings with instructor and/or tutors are always welcomed.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Christian Ethics
courses.description: This course examines the context, nature, method and principles of Christian ethics. Emphasis is given to contemporary challenges to and of Christian ethics.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Divinity
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. To understand the diversities of Christian ethics and its challenges.
2. To acquire students with knowledge to critically reflect the contemporary issues.
3. To make appropriate critical connections between the study of Christian ethics, practical theology and biblical studies.
4. To appreciate the impact that one’s values and decisions make on the lives of oneself and others.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Critiques of morality
2. Religion and morality
3. Theories of Ethics
4. Foundations (1): Jesus Christ and Christian ethics
5. Foundations (2): The Bible and Christian ethics
6. Foundations (3): Natural law and Christian ethics
7. Foundations (4): Religious life and practice
8. Foundations (5): History of Christian Ethics
9. Moral agent (1) : The Church
10. Moral agent (2) : Spirituality and Morality
11. Christian ethics in contexts (1): Different models of Christian ethics
12. Christian ethics in contexts (2): Contemporary challenges
13. Christian ethics in contexts (3): Chinese religious context
courses.feedback_evaluation: An end-of-term university-wide course evaluation will be conducted in class. Students’ comments and feedback on the course through e-mail or personal meetings with instructor and/or tutors are always welcomed.

courses.learning_outcomes: Students will acquire the knowledge, skills, and attitude required to conduct in-depth research of his chosen topic.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Church Organist Training
courses.description: The class discusses the skills and techniques of hymn playing and anthem accompanying on the organ. Students will learn how to combine organ repertoire into church liturgies, and also explore different styles of service playing from the Baroque period to the modern time. Students can take this course for up to 3 terms (total of 3 credits). Pianists are welcomed to register for the course but an audition will be required.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Divinity
courses.learning_outcomes:
The student is expected to develop a high proficiency in music making, and be able to lead a worship service through music. Throughout the term, the student will look into a special topic that is related to the history of organ/ service playing (e.g. registrations of Bach chorale preludes, French Organ Mass of the 17th Century, Tradition of Hymns and Anthems Playing in England in the 19th Century, etc)

The student should also be self-motivated and practise the assigned organ pieces diligently on a daily basis.
courses.course_syllabus:
Depending on the technique, sight-reading and aural skill of individual student, here are some topics to be explored by the student (student working on different areas simultaneously each week) throughout the time of their organ study.

Area A:

On Hymn Playing

- improvising an introduction for hymn

- improvising a prelude or postlude on a given hymn tune

- reharmonizing hymn tunes

- improvising on interludes

- transposing to a new key for a hymn

- choosing appropriate registrations, tempi for hymns

- knowing the background the hymn text and hymn writers

Area B:

Anthem Playing

- be able to watch the conducting patterns

- be able to work with instrumentalists and singers

- conducting and playing at the console

- transcribing from piano scores

- changing registrations while accompanying on the organ

- choosing appropriate registrations for anthems

Area C:

Organ Literature and Repertoire

- history of the organ

- history of organ playing in church worships

- organ tablatures

- organ repertoire from the Medieval Period- Contemporary

- history of organ building

- different schools (e.g. North German style, French Romantic) of organ repertoire

- historical methods

- different genres (e.g. German Pareludium, German chorale-preludes, French organ mass)
courses.feedback_evaluation: Course evaluation form
courses.required_readings:
Ritchie, George and B. Stauffer. Organ Technique: Modern and Early. 2000. (Oxford)

Laukviv, Jon. Historical Performance Practice in Organ Playing. 1996. (Carus-Verlag)

Hymns and organ pieces from the Renaissance to our modern days.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Methods and Paradigms of Christian Studies
courses.description: This course aims at providing students with a solid methodological and theoretical understanding of the multidisciplinary study of Christianity. It mainly introduces the students to the principal tasks (e.g., historical, constructive, practical) and methods, together with their theoretical presuppositions, of Christian studies. It also acquaints students with major theological paradigms (e.g., traditional, liberal, liberationist, feminist, etc.) and their different visions of the theological disciplines (e.g., biblical studies, history of Christianity, systematic theology, pastoral studies, etc.). Issues such as the relation of Christian studies to other academic fields, the alleged conflict between its scientific and ecclesial orientations, and the interconnection of its diverse disciplines, will be discussed.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA in Christian Studies and MA in Religious Studies programmes.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. To obtain a broadened and deepened understanding of the major traditions of Christianity in the world in historical and comparative perspectives
2. To be able to understand major theological paradigms in Christianity with their internal complexity, and be able to extend such understanding to issues in church and society
3. To have some knowledge of the tasks and methods of the major disciplines in Christian studies and be able to apply such knowledge in research
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction. Christianity as Multi- Paradigmatic.
2. Historical-Theological Paradigms (1): Early Jewish-Christian Apocalyptic; Patristic Hellenistic Orthodox
3. Historical-Theological Paradigms (2): Mediaeval Latin Roman Catholic; Reformation Protestant.
4. Theological Research and Paper Writing
5. The Tasks and Methods of Biblical Studies
6. The Tasks and Methods of History of Christianity
7. Historical-Theological Paradigms (3): Modernity; Liberal Theology; Neo-Orthodox Theology.
8. Historical-Theological Paradigms (4): Evangelicalism; Pentecostalism
9. The Tasks and Methods of Systematic Theology
10. Historical-Theological Paradigms (5): Liberation Theology; Feminist Theology.
11. The Tasks and Methods of Practical Theology
12. Global Christianity in the 21st century
13. Interdisciplinary approaches to Christian Studies
courses.feedback_evaluation:
Feedback and recommendation are welcome. Students are advised to give feedback either
during the lectures or through email. Response will be made promptly according to the need
of students.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: New Testament in Contexts
courses.description: This course will select some texts of the New Testament with contemporary methods for in-depth study. Texts include selected themes of the Pauline Epistles, Synoptic Gospels and Johannine Literature, e.g., Pauline Epistle Form, Doctrinal and Ethical Teachings; Eucharist, Baptism, Miracles and Teachings of the Synoptics; as well as Revelation structure, “I am saying”, Farewell Discourse of the Gospel of John.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Exclusion=Not for students who have taken THEO5312 Introduction to the New Testament
For students in MA Religious Studies or MA Christian Studies
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students should be able to:
Expected Knowledge Outcomes
1. Know how to find reliable and trustworthy resources for their own reconstruction of socio-historical contexts of the New Testament;
2. Compare and contrast New Testament studies in public domain with uncritical voices by means of established paradigms and academic consensus;
Expected Skills Outcome
3. Analyze context(s) behind primary texts and evaluate their relevance in biblical exegeses; (\* This also serve as a preparation of key research skills for graduate work in advanced biblical studies)
Expected Attitude Outcome
4. Deepen their critical and yet sympathetic understanding of earliest Christian traditions in our contemporary contexts and appraise their contributions in shaping different cultures.
courses.course_syllabus:
• Matrix and Methods (NT as sacred text and historical source, social historical contexts of Judaism
and Greco-Roman culture in the first century, textual criticism, source criticism, form criticism,
redaction criticism, narrative criticism, sociological approach, ideology criticism, history of
effects)
• The Four Gospels (Mark, Matthew, Luke, John; literary design and social historical contexts,
Christology and discipleship)
• Paul’s Letters (Philemon, First Thessalonians, First Corinthians, Romans, Galatians; contexts and
contents; crises and messages)
• Other Writings (Pastoral Epistles, Peter’s Letters, Hebrews, Revelation; hostile contexts, church
formation in the world, hope beyond now)
courses.feedback_evaluation:
• Students are encouraged to share their learning experience throughout the course.
• Student’s weekly oral presentations of critical review and exegesis research in class provide concrete and early feedback for

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Methods and Biblical Interpretation
courses.description: Biblical interpretation does not happen in a vacuum. Every interpretive act involves the text, the reader, and its signifying context, including that of the writer(s) and that of the reader, defined by the complex web of social, cultural, political, and even psychological forces. This course covers some major methods used in the critical study of the Bible (with a focus on the Hebrew Bible/Old Testament) from the late nineteenth century to the early twenty-first century, including the historical-critical methods, literary criticism, and reader-centered approaches. It examines the tasks, presuppositions, and reading strategies of various exegetical methods, and evaluates each’s weaknesses and strengths.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA in Christian Studies, Master of Divinity
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students should be able to:
• Describe and compare various critical methods of biblical studies
• Deepen their awareness of how the writer’s and interpreter’s social, cultural, political, and even psychological contexts may affect the process of signification
• Demonstrate a familiarity of the current approaches to biblical interpretation
• Be able to apply at least seven of the critical methods in their reading of the biblical texts
• Be able to formulate their own intersectional and interdisciplinary approach to biblical interpretation
courses.course_syllabus:
Topics:
Week 1) Introduction
Week 2) 1. Historical Criticism, 2. Textual Criticism
Week 3) 3. Source Criticism, 4. Form Criticism
Week 4) 5. Tradition-Historical Criticism, 6. Redaction Criticism
Week 5) 7. Canonical Approach, 8. Folkloristics
Week 6) 9. Rhetorical Analysis, 10. Rhetorical (or Communicative) Criticism
Week 7) 11. Structuralist Criticism, 12. Deconstructive Criticism
Week 8) 13. Reader-Response Criticism, 14. Ideological Criticism
Week 9) 15. Feminist (and Womanist) Criticism, 16. Cultural Criticism and Cross-Textual (and Cross-Cultural) Hermeneutics
Week 10) 17. Social-Scientific Criticism, 18. Socio-Rhetorical Interpretation
Week 11) 19. Narrative Criticism and Narratology
Week 12) 20. Postcolonial Criticism, 21. Queer (or Gender) Criticism
Week 13) 22. Psychological Criticism, Conclusion
courses.feedback_evaluation: Two course evaluations will be conducted. The first will be done midway through the course which is tailored to facilitate better progress through the course. The second will be conducted by the university wide exercise.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: The Hebrew Bible in Contexts
courses.description: "This course aims at introducing students to the approaches to reading the Hebrew Bible (The Christian Old Testament) in its literary, socio-political and religio-cultural contexts. The literary setting and historical background will be surveyed and explored in order to enhance students’ understanding of the text and the dynamics of the reading process."
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Exclusion=Not for students who have taken THEO5311 Introduction to the Hebrew Bible
For students in MA Christian Studies
courses.learning_outcomes:
Knowledge Outcomes
(1) Describe
a. what the study of Hebrew Bible constitutes.
b. with the help of resources, the general background and contexts of the biblical books.
(2) Explain
a. the necessity of understanding contexts of when studying the Hebrew Bible.
b. the literary, socio-political and religio-cultural contexts of a specified period in biblical history.
c. the necessity and limitations in biblical scholarship for reconstructing contexts.
(3) Distinguish
a. what is “historical Israel” and “biblical Israel”.
b. the different contexts for interpreting scriptures.
(4) Recite the main historical epochs of biblical Israel.
(5) Examine and analyse the contexts of a given text using quality resources.
(6) Sketch an interpretation of a given text integrating a current context to a passage in the Hebrew Bible.
(7) Comment and evaluate on those information that were presented differently in church. Determine the consequences of the information.
(8) Reflect and discuss on the implications of academic study and its impact on faith-based communities.
(9) Construct interpretations that take the contexts of the biblical text/s seriously and also relevant to one’s context.
Attitude Outcomes:
(1) Appreciate by listing the contributions of the diverse interpretations of scriptures throughout history and their historical and contextual developments.
(2) Respect for the historical development of Old Testament scholarship in its entirety by pointing out their relevance to current contexts – within the communities of faith and also within the academia.
(3) Develop keen insights to read texts meaningfully and responsibly by reading up and constructing different contextual interpretations of biblical texts – for the sake of peace and justice, understanding and acceptance to all humanity and living creatures of God’s creation.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Course Introduction: The Awareness of the Context(s); OT vs. HB Canon
2. Ancient Contexts: Archaeology and Mythology (Genesis)
3. Ancient Contexts: History, History Writing and Historiography (Exodus)
4. The Nature of Criticism (Leviticus and Numbers)
5. Ancient Context: Literary Convention in the Ancient World (Samuel-Saul-David)
6. Ancient Contexts: The Empire and the Exile (Second Temple literature)
7. Ancient Contexts: Social studies and Socio-Economic Contexts (the Book of the Twelve)
8. Contemporary Contexts: Modern mind as context (Joshua and Judges; SOS)
9. Contemporary Contexts: Post-Modernity as context (Job and Qohelet)
10. Contemporary Contexts: Judaism and the Church as context; Term Paper Proposal (Ruth)
11. Contemporary Contexts: The Public Space as context (Jeremiah)
12. The Reception of the Texts (Isaiah)
13. A Retrospect and a proposal
courses.feedback_evaluation: Two course evaluations will be conducted. The first will be done midway through the course, which is tailored to facilitate better progress through the course. The second one will be conducted by the university-wide exercise.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Chinese Christianities
courses.description: This course examines various issues regarding the course of Christianity indigenization and contextualization in China, such as the development of Nestorian Christianity in Tang Dynasty, Jesuits mission and the Rites Controversy, the issue of Chinese Bible translation and Christian literature, Christianity and social reform and reconstruction in China, independent churches and evangelists in China, and the issue of Christianity and races. By discussing the above issues, this course shall explore how the Chinese experience would contribute to the enrichment of church universal.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Divinity
courses.learning_outcomes:
Students are expected to, by the end of the course,
1. Show their concrete knowledge of certain terms and concepts, such as indigenization, contextualisation, localisation, inculturation and interculturation.
2. Be able to critically reflect on issues regarding Christianity indigenization in China from historical perspectives.
3. Demonstrate their abilities to reflect on and evaluate, from theological perspectives, the efforts towards Christianity indigenization by Chinese Christians.
courses.course_syllabus:
一、基督教 與 中國 的相遇 「本色化」與「中國基督教」
二、 「 十字蓮花 」 東方教會在中國
三、「禮儀之爭」：明末天主教的中國「適應」
四、基督新教 傳教士的中文述著與聖經翻譯問題
五、本色神學議題（一）：中國宗教與基督教
六、本色神學議題（二）：耶儒之間
七、本色神學議題（三）：處境實踐 I
八、本色神學議題（三）：處境實踐 II
九、本色教會議題（一）：自立運動與本土教派 I
十、本色教會議題（一）：自立運動與本土教派 II 專題 講座
十一、本色教會議題（二）：禮儀、節期與詩歌
十二、總結 ：多元化的中國基督教
courses.feedback_evaluation: An end-of-term university-wide course evaluation will be conducted in class. Students’ comments and feedback on the course through e-mail or personal meetings with instructor and/or tutors are always welcomed.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Christianity and Chinese Culture
courses.description: Starting from the traditional understanding of indigenization whereby culture are often looked upon as static entities, Christianity and Chinese culture will also be compared as they moved through the recent history of China as two living forces competing to shape the China that was to be. This is an open-ended course which will continuously be informed by recent trends and developments.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Religious Studies or Master of Divinity or MA Christian Studies
courses.course_syllabus:
〈第1講〉基督教與中國文化：不同層次的相遇
〈第2講〉中國文化結構(1)：經濟結構、政治社會結構、意識形態結構
〈第3講〉中國文化結構(2)：現代化與中國文化結構的轉型
〈第4講〉中國文化的文化神學(1)：政治文化的基督教反思
〈第5講〉中國文化的文化神學(2)： 政治經濟的基督教反思—「社會主義」社會理想、「資本主義」社會理想與人性的建構
〈第6講〉中國的身體、心靈與人際關係(1)：從中國人生哲學到中國人的國民性格
〈第7講〉中國的身體、心靈與人際關係(2)：人情、關係、面子的權力結構分析
〈第8講〉中國的身體、心靈與人際關係(3)：《孫子兵法》與基督教人際關係倫理學的反思
〈第9講〉中國的身體、心靈與人際關係(4)：《黃帝內經》與基督教的養生與養心
courses.feedback_evaluation: An end-of-term university-wide course evaluation will be conducted in class. Students’ comments and feedback on the course through e-mail or personal meetings with instructor and/or tutors are always welcomed.
courses.required_readings:
1. 香港城市大學中國文化科目中心編：《中國文化導讀》(香港城市大學中國文化課程)(香港：香港城市大學出版社1999)。
2. 金觀濤：《在歷史的表象背後》(台北：谷風出版社，1983)。
3. 秦家懿、孔漢思(Hans Küng)合著：《中國宗教與西方神學》(Christianity and Chinese Religions)(台北：聯經出版事業公司1989)。
courses.recommended_readings: Further readings will be provided alongside lecture contents. Please consult the course teacher(s) for the most up-to-date reading list.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Media, Popular Culture and Christianity
courses.description: This course is a critical exploration of the intricate relationships between popular media culture and Christianity. While popular culture and the media have long become a daily reality for many in the world, these have seldom been taken seriously by theologians and scholars of religion. Also, the relationship between popular media and religion (including Christianity) is often overlooked in academic studies until relatively recent time. We shall re-examine various manifestations of popular culture from Christian theological perspectives, and also probes into the subtle interrelatedness between media culture and Christianity as an organised religion. As its wider academic context, this course engages with the interdisciplinary field of media, religion, and culture (MRC) which has been under vigorous development since the mid-1990s; as its immediate socio-cultural-religious context of discussions, it attends particularly to the Chinese language popular media and Chinese Christianity in Hong Kong.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA in Christian Studies, Master of Divinity and MA in Religious Studies programmes
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Why media, popular culture, and Christianity?

2. Regarding media and popular culture: what popular media cultures are and how they have been studied

3. Reviewing Hong Kong popular culture

4. Relating Media, Popular Culture, and Christianity

5. Images and visual culture – religious and theological dimensions

6. Engaging with images theologically

7. Television, society, and the construction of identities

8. Cinema, narratives, and the cultural turn of theology

9. Advertising, consumerism, and the expressions of faith

10. Google, Facebook, and the revolution in human social existence

11. News, perception, and the reconstruction of reality

12. Renewing Christian Praxis in Media Cultures

13. Re-envisioning Christian Theology in an Age of Mediated Cultures
courses.feedback_evaluation:
Course questionnaire

Reflection of teacher (including evidence from assessment)
courses.required_readings:
Foundational texts of the course would include the following, plus additional reading materials that are relevant to various specific topics to be discussed:

Cobb, Kelton. The Blackwell Guide to Theology and Popular Culture. Blackwell, 2005.

Hunter, James Davison. To Change the World: the Irony, Tragedy, and possibility of Christianity in the Late Modern World. Oxford, 2010.

Lynch, Gordon. Understanding Theology and Popular Culture. Blackwell, 2005.

Lynch, Gordon, ed. Between Sacred and Profane: Researching Religion and Popular Culture. I.B. Tauris, 2007.

Mitchell, Jolyon. Media Violence and Christian Ethics. Cambridge University Press, 2007.

Mitcell, Jolyon and Sophia Marriage, eds. Mediating Religion: Conversations in Media, Religion and Culture. T&T Clark, 2003.

Morgan, David, ed. Key Words in Religion, Media and Culture. Routledge, 2008.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Theological Studies: Seminar I
courses.description: Discussion in seminar setting on specific issues related to Theological Studies. Students are allowed to take these courses for more than once, and gain the units each time they pass the course. The specific content will be designed by the teacher who offers the courses. Students are allowed to take these courses for more than once.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Divinity
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. To critically review, consolidate, and extend theological knowledge with special reference to one or more selected topics or issues.
2. To analyze, evaluate, and/or synthesize theological ideas, concepts, and perspectives to address critically and constructively certain theological issues.
3. To deepen the understanding of the diversity and complexity of theological positions and perspectives and to make informed judgment in theological studies.
courses.course_syllabus: Supervisor will provide specific guidance according to student’s research topic.
courses.feedback_evaluation: End-of-term university wide course evaluation will be conducted. On-and-off student feedback will be received by supervisor.
courses.required_readings:
Bevans, Stephen B. *Models of Contextual Theology*. Rev. and Expanded ed. Faith and Cultures Series. Maryknoll, N.Y.: Orbis Books, 2002.
Thompson, Judith, Stephen Pattison and Ross Thompson. *Theological Reflection*. London: SCM Press, 2008.
Veeneman, Mary M. *Introducing Theological Method: A Survey of Contemporary Theologians and Approaches*. Grand Rapids: Baker Academic, 2017.
courses.recommended_readings:
Bergmann, Sigurd. *God in Context: A Survey of Contextual Theology*. Florence: Routledge, 2003.
Browning, Don S. *A Fundamental Practical Theology: Descriptive and Strategic Proposals*. Minneapolis: Fortress Press, 1996.
Gill, Robin and et al. *Theology in a Social Context: Sociological Theology*. Farnham: Taylor & Francis Group, 2012.
Hallett, Garth. *Theology within the Bounds of Language: A Methodological Tour*. Albany: State University of New York Press, 2011.
Milbank, John. *Theology and Social Theory: Beyond Secular Reason*. Hoboken: John Wiley & Sons, 2006.
Swinton, John, and Harriet Mowat. *Practical Theology and Qualitative Research*. 2n ed. London: SCM Press, 2016.

Other required readings will be suggested by supervisor based on individual student’s concentration of area studies.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Contemporary Spirituality
courses.description: A study of the history, tradition, practice and various forms of spirituality in contemporary Christian life.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Divinity or MA Christian Studies
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction to Christian Spirituality: Historical Overview and Classics 基督教靈修學導論：歷史發展及經典作品
2. Biblical Spirituality《聖經》中的靈修學
3. Desert Fathers and Monasticism 沙漠教父與修道主義
4. Eastern Orthodox Spirituality 東正教的靈修傳統
5. Spiritual Experiences and Practices of St. Francis of Assisi 聖方濟的屬靈經歷與操練
6. The Carmelite: St. Teresa of Avila, St. John of the Cross, St. Thérèse of Lisieux 聖衣會傳
統：大德蘭、聖若望十架、小德蘭
7. The Jesuits and Spiritual Exercises 耶穌會的屬靈操練
8. Protestant Spirituality 基督新教的屬靈
9. Spiritual Experiences of Charismatic Movement 靈恩運動的屬靈體驗
10. Spirituality in a Busy World 在繁忙生活中的靈修
courses.feedback_evaluation: An end-of-term university-wide course evaluation will be conducted in class. Students’ comments and feedback on the course through e-mail or personal meetings with instructor and/or tutors are always welcomed.
courses.required_readings:
1. Jones, Cheslyn, Wainwright, Geoffrey, and Yarnold, Edward, ed. The Study of Spirituality. New York: Oxford University Press, 1986.
2. Mursell, Gordon., ed. The Story of Christian Spirituality: Two Thousand Years, from East to West. Minneapolis: Fortress, 2001.
3. 傅士德（Richard J. Foster）著：《屬靈傳統禮讚》（Streams of Living Waters）（香港：基道書樓，2001）。
4. Wakefield, Gordon S., ed. The Westminster Dictionary of Christian Spirituality. Philadelphia: The Westminster Press, 1983.
5. 彭順強：《二千年靈修神學歷史》（香港：天道，2005）。
6. Kaisch, Ken. Finding God: A Handbook of Christian Meditation. New York: Paulist Press, 1994.
courses.recommended_readings: Further readings will be provided alongside lecture contents. Please consult the course teacher(s) for the most up-to-date reading list.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Christian Counselling
courses.description: This course will cover the following areas: 1) the similarities and differences between Christian Counselling and General Counselling; 2) the similarities and differences between Pastoral Counselling and Pastoral Care; 3) different approaches to Christian Counselling; and 4) skills, knowledge and practical competence in Christian Counselling.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Divinity or MA Christian Studies
courses.course_syllabus:
Introduction
Christian Counseling vs. General Counseling
Negotiating a Presence-Centred Christian Counselling
Revisiting Empathy
Reframing as a technique
Hope-based counseling
Faith healing, hope, and religious ritual
Sin-based counseling
o Counseling & individual sin
o Counseling & sinned against
Selected topic 1: Crisis counseling
Selected topic 2: Suffering
Selected topic 3: Mindfulness Practices for Christian Counseling
Case illustration
Bereavement counseling
o Disenfranchised grief
o Continuing bond
courses.feedback_evaluation: An end-of-term university-wide course evaluation will be conducted in class. Students’ comments and feedback on the course through e-mail or personal meetings with instructor and/or tutors are always welcomed.
courses.required_readings:
Kwan, Simon Shui-Man. 2016. Negotiating a Presence-Centred Christian Counselling: Towards a Theologically
Informed and Culturally Sensitive Approach. Newcastle, UK: CSP.
Additional Readings: As assigned and distributed in class sessions.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Theology and Counseling Theories
courses.description: This course serves two basic functions: first, to equip students with a basic understanding on the contemporary general counseling theories: second, to enhance students’ competence to read these theories theologically. The methodology adopted will be a kind of mutually critical correlation. Substantively, the course will introduce six to seven contemporary counseling theories, which will be followed by theological critiques on them.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Divinity or MA Christian Studies
courses.learning_outcomes:
Aims: The core of this course is built around the interface between theology and “secular” (or general) counseling. It aims to
1. familiarize students with a range of therapeutic models;
2. enhance students’ competence to read these models theologically.
Learning Outcomes:
1. Students gain basic understanding on several models of secular counseling;
2. Students learn the critical skills to theologize counseling theories with a mutually critical correlational perspective.
3. Students start to appreciate the possibilities of integration between social scientific theories and theological traditions.
4. Students gain a basic understanding of the meaning and how-to of reflective practice.
courses.course_syllabus:
Introduction I
1. Introduction: class discussion on Soul and Afterlife
Introduction II
2. Models of Integration between Theology and Secular Sciences
A Recent Trend
3. On “Common Factors Debate”—therapy as religious experience and God as the Healer
Theological reflection on therapeutic models
4. On Person Centered Therapy—Acceptance and God’s Nature
5. On REBT—the experiential language of sin
6. On Gestalt therapy—Theological Reflection on “Presence”
7. On Logo-Therapy—Meaning of Suffering
8. On some forms of healing associated with the New Age Movement (Family Constellation & Hypnosis)—Theology and Paranormality/Parapsychology
9. Narrativity in Counseling and the Problem of the Self—the theology of Imago Dei
Selected Topics
10. Mindfulness, Neuroplasticity and the theology of Religious Experience
11. Intellectual Disability (or Mental Retardation), Theology of Disability, and Theology & Practice of Conversion
12. Class Discussion
courses.feedback_evaluation: An end-of-term university-wide course evaluation will be conducted in class. Students’ comments and feedback on the course through e-mail or personal meetings with instructor and/or tutors are always welcomed.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Theological Studies: Seminar II
courses.description: Discussion in seminar setting on specific issues related to Theological Studies. Students are allowed to take these courses for more than once, and gain the units each time they pass the course. The specific content will be designed by the teacher who offers the courses. Students are allowed to take these courses for more than once.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Divinity
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. To critically review, consolidate, and extend theological knowledge with special reference to one or more selected topics or issues.
2. To analyze, evaluate, and/or synthesize theological ideas, concepts, and perspectives to address critically and constructively certain theological issues.
3. To deepen the understanding of the diversity and complexity of theological positions and perspectives and to make informed judgment in theological studies.
courses.course_syllabus: Supervisor will provide specific guidance according to student’s research topic.
courses.feedback_evaluation: End-of-term university wide course evaluation will be conducted. On-and-off student feedback will be received by supervisor.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Special Topics on Biblical Studies I
courses.description: This course will examine topics or problems related to Biblical Studies, such as: Pentateuchal Studies, Prophetic Literature, Wisdom and the Psalms, Synoptic Gospels, Johannine Literature, Pauline Letters, Hermeneutics, sociological approaches and historical background of the biblical studies, etc. Students are allowed to take these courses for more than once.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Divinity or MA Christian Studies or MA Theological Studies
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completing the course, students should be able to:
1. List the components and describe the complexity of the Biblical Topic.
2. Review past scholarship and comment how the Topic was dealt with.
3. Analyse and evaluate the Topic from various perspectives, such as: New Criticisms of Biblical Interpretations and/or Contextual Interpretations of the Bible.
4. Research on and theorise the relationship between the Topic and the theology or other theological disciplines.
5. Reflect and articulate how this Topic can be relevant in the various contexts.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Special Topics on Mission I
courses.description: The course examines topics that arise in the broader field of mission studies, such as the history of mission, present-day mission movements, theology of mission, mission and evangelism among special people groups, intercultural theology, or theology of religions.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Divinity and MA in Theological Studies
courses.learning_outcomes:
a) Analytic understanding of different historical and contemporary mission paradigms and patterns;
b) Synthetic ability to design a well reflected and practical plan for the church's mission to a specific people group
courses.course_syllabus:
Course syllabus example: Mission in the context of Asia

Part I Backgrounds

Week 1 Introduction, course outline, assignments. What is mission

Week 2 The socio-religious context of Asia

Week 3 Mission history of Asia (I)

Week 4 Mission history of Asia (II)

Part II Present-day mission movements

Week 5 Mission methods to so-called Creative Access Nations: China, Myanmar, North-Korea, Vietnam, and others

Week 6 China: some contemporary mission examples

Week 7 Mission in the context of Islam: Central Asia and the Middle East. Strategies, examples

Week 8 Mission in the context of Buddhism: Thailand, Myanmar, Sri Lanka

Week 9 Mission in the context of Hinduism: India, Nepal

Week 10 Poverty and struggles for social justice in the Asia-Pacific Rim

Week 11 Mission in the context of the city

Week 12 Student presentations

Week 13 Students presentations

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Theology and Hong Kong Society
courses.description: This course equips students with knowledge and skills in theologizing with an interdisciplinary horizon across a variety of aspects of everyday practical life in Hong Kong. It helps students to see how different sub-disciplines of theology can be critically integrated into a coordinated whole and in this way be relevant to the practical life in one's own context. Social and theological analysis are given equal emphasis, and how these two could be mutually and critically correlated will be demonstrated. Experts or/and experienced practitioners in different fields of practice will be invited to share with students their analyses of the respective public/current issues, while theological responses (philosophical, exegetical, historical, and practical) will be given by teachers from different sub-disciplines in theology. Around half of the lectures will be structured in the form of an interdisciplinary dialogue.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Pre-requisite = THEO5906 Methods and Paradigms of Christian Studies or THEO5336 Systematic Theology II
courses.learning_outcomes:
At the completion of this course, students should be able to achieve the below:

Knowledge Aspect

1. Acquire a general but critical framework to integrate various sub-disciplines of theology for a practical analysis and evaluation of the Hong Kong society.

2. Demonstrate a contextual understanding of a variety of models of publicization of theology.

Skill Aspect

1. Acquire the ability to apply an interdisciplinary approach to a theological analysis and evaluation of the Hong Kong society in some selected aspects (please refer to Course Syllabus).

2. Begin to be able to formulate one's own initial interdisciplinary framework to do contextual theological analyses of one's own place.

Values and Attitudes Aspect

1. Appreciate the importance of an interdisciplinary horizon to any theological understanding of one's own society.

2. Critically aware of the role of theology in public sphere of one's own place.

3. Appreciate the value of defragmentation of theological study.
courses.course_syllabus:
This course will be composed of two core sections.

Core Section 1: Three Major Lenses or Models for understanding the possible ways for theology to engage the public sphere will be introduced briefly. The principal aim of this section is to equip the students with basic frameworks to understand and engage in the dialogical platform that constitutes the second core section.

1. Introduction:

a. David Tracy's "theology as public discourse for public realm" as the theological orientation of Christian faith and praxis.

b. Three kinds of publicness that mark theological discourse.

c. David Tracy’s notion of “the public at large” and its relationship with practical theology.

d. The tradition of the public relevance of theology.

e. A practical-prophetic ecclesiology.

2. Public Theology as a Dialogical Lens

a. What is public theology?

b. Models of public theology as advanced by Eneida Jacobsen.

c. Local examples.

3. Practical Theology as a Dialogical Lens

a. Practical theology as a transformative discourse

b. The correlational relationship between different theological sub-movements (descriptive, historical, systematic, and strategic practical) and praxis.

c. Types of practical-theological reflection as advanced by Elaine Graham, Heather Walton, and Frances Ward.

d. Local examples.

4. Political Theology and Liberation Theology as Dialogical Lenses

a. History and types of political theology and liberation theology.

b. Political theology and liberation theology as critical-prophetic witness of the church.

c. Emancipation and liberation as core themes for theologizing.

d. Local examples.

Core Section 2: Interdisciplinary Dialogical Platform to experiment on the ways for theology to engage concrete public issues. Around five public/current issues from the below area-list will be selected each year for the dialogue. Experts or/and experienced practitioners in different fields of practice will be invited to the platform to share with students their analyses of the respective public/current issues, while theological responses (philosophical, exegetical, historical, and practical) will be given by teachers from different sub-disciplines in theology. Students are expected to engage in the dialogue with the theories and concepts they learnt from core section 1, and with their own experience as a citizen. They will also be required to continue the discussion on the eLearning platform (Blackboard) which constitutes one of their homework assignments.

1. Land Use in Hong Kong; and, Land in the Hebrew Bible: The dialogue will focus on issues related to sustainability, conservation, development, environmental justice, and theologies of territory in the Hebrew Bible.

2. Poverty and Justice in Hong Kong; and, Church and the Struggle for Social Justice: The dialogue will focus on (a) the different ways (social scientific and theological) of describing, categorizing, explaining, and interpreting the phenomenon of poverty in Hong Kong; (b) how can the church build a movement of Christians committed to tackling poverty in Hong Kong?

3. Sex, Marriage and Family in Hong Kong; and, En-Gendering Theology: LGBT issues in Hong Kong will be critically discussed. Different ways of Christian responses will be examined.

4. Mainland-Hong Kong Relations; and Church-State Relations: The changing Mainland-HK relations amid integration and confrontation will be discussed. Beijing’s policy toward HK and the local reactions in the midst of socio-economic integration in the recent decade will be the focus. This changing phenomenon will be critically engaged with various theological perspectives of church-state relations.

5. Rule of Law in Hong Kong; and Rule of Law and Christian Traditions: “Is Hong Kong’s rule of law under threat?” “How is the rule of law to be understood, and understood in the local context?” “How shall we understand the biblical foundations for the rule of law in the Western world?” “How shall Christian communities respond to the concerned local debates?” These questions will form the focal concern of the dialogue.

6. Selected issues in Social Policy; and Christian Vision of a Good Society: one concrete issue regarding local social policy on housing, labor, social welfare, medical and health care, education, or social security, etc. will be selected for the dialogue. How Christian visions of a good society can engage in the debate will be examined.

7. Other Important Public Affairs that take place during the Teaching Period

courses.required_readings:
1 Introduction: Theology as Public Discourse for Public Realm

• Required Readings:

• Gill, Robin. Society Shaped by Theology. Farnham: Ashgate, 2013. Chs. 1 & 9.

• Mathewes, Charles T. 2007. "A Theology of Public Life." In A Theology of Public Life, ed. Charles T. Mathewes, ch. 17. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.

• Tracy, David. 1975. "Theology as Public Discourse." Christian Century 92: 280-4.

• Tracy, David. 2014. "Three Kinds of Publicness in Public Theology." International Journal of Public Theology 8 (3): 330-4.

2 Public Theology as a Dialogical Lens

• Required Readings:

• Breitenberg, E. Harold. 2010. "What Is Public Theology." In Public Theology for a Global Society: Essays in Honor of Max L. Stackhouse, eds. Deirdre King Hainsworth and Scott Paeth, 3-17. Grand Rapids, Mich.; Cambridge: Eerdmans.

• Required Readings:

• Graham, Elaine L., Heather Walton, and Frances Ward. 2005. Theological Reflection: Methods. London: SCM. Pp. 1-17.

courses.subject: THEO
courses.title: Pastoral Studies: Seminar I
courses.description: A study in depth of one or more topics and issues arising in pastoral studies and Christian nurture, such as counselling in special occasions, liturgies, local church and community, lay training, church growth, Christian stewardship, etc. Students are allowed to take these courses for more than once, and gain the units each time they pass the course.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in Master of Divinity
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. To critically review, consolidate, and extend knowledge with special reference to one or more selected topics or issues on pastoral studies.
2. To analyze, evaluate, and/or synthesize ideas, concepts, and perspectives to address critically and constructively certain issues on pastoral studies.
3. To deepen the understanding of the diversity and complexity of theological positions and perspectives and to make informed judgment in pastoral studies.
